coredata.dc:description: Biofuels' sustainable energy necessitates using corrosion-resistant steels in plant equipment and storage tanks. However, the availability of locally sourced PCC (precipitated calcium carbonate) from Buton asphalt-recovered solid wastes (ashbuton) promises technically and economically for Zn-PCC composites coated on mild carbon steel grade in biodiesel storage tank construction. This study was to investigate electrodeposited mild steel with Zn and Zn-PCC composites followed by corrosion testing on uncoated and coated steel samples in a simulated biodiesel environment employing the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique. The results indicated that Zn-PCC (ashbuton) coated to the mild steel greatly enhanced the substrate's corrosion performance in terms of polarization resistance (4204.6 Ω), lowest corrosion rate (2.1557E-7 mm/year), and through EIS analysis shows the corrosion rates in the following sequence: Zn-PCC (commercial) > Zn > Zn-PCC (ashbuton). Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) results of selected produced coatings revealed the presence of homogeneous calcium carbonates and a lesser amount of iron oxide in the mild steel coating. The Zn-PCC coating is ideal for industrial biodiesel storage tanks and uses the low-cost value of PCC-derived industrial wastes to improve corrosion resistance.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biofuels' sustainable energy necessitates using corrosion-resistant steels in plant equipment and storage tanks. However, the availability of locally sourced PCC (precipitated calcium carbonate) from Buton asphalt-recovered solid wastes (ashbuton) promises technically and economically for Zn-PCC composites coated on mild carbon steel grade in biodiesel storage tank construction. This study was to investigate electrodeposited mild steel with Zn and Zn-PCC composites followed by corrosion testing on uncoated and coated steel samples in a simulated biodiesel environment employing the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique. The results indicated that Zn-PCC (ashbuton) coated to the mild steel greatly enhanced the substrate's corrosion performance in terms of polarization resistance (4204.6 Ω), lowest corrosion rate (2.1557E-7 mm/year), and through EIS analysis shows the corrosion rates in the following sequence: Zn-PCC (commercial) > Zn > Zn-PCC (ashbuton). Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) results of selected produced coatings revealed the presence of homogeneous calcium carbonates and a lesser amount of iron oxide in the mild steel coating. The Zn-PCC coating is ideal for industrial biodiesel storage tanks and uses the low-cost value of PCC-derived industrial wastes to improve corrosion resistance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy availability and efficiency by replacing fossil fuels with renewable energy sources are key factors in sustainable energy development globally (Sreeharsha et al., 2023; Doğan et al., 2022). In particular, environmental pollution (Kheiralipour et al., 2024) and energy supply are becoming issues worldwide due to limited energy resources and the increasing price of crude fossil fuels (Wu et al., 2023). In addition to the high consumption of fossil fuels, their use has increased greenhouse gas emissions into the environment, contributing to global warming (Osman et al., 2023). Switching to renewable energy in biodiesel reduces global warming while improving public health, the environment, and long-term energy sustainability (Rial, 2024). As a result, while biodiesel holds promise as an environmentally friendly fuel, monitoring how well biodiesel handling and storage equipment performs is challenging due to biodiesel's corrosion susceptibility, which relates to its chemical characteristics such as water content, dissolved oxygen, and acidity (Koch et al., 2016; Koch and El-Sherik, 2017; Popoola et al., 2013; Ivošević et al., 2022). Biodiesels produced using different raw ingredients and preparation processes can have varying corrosion performance.

In addition to corrosiveness (Kugelmeier et al., 2021; Fazal et al., 2013), oxidation (Wang et al., 2021; Fazal et al., 2017), fuel property instability (Fazal et al., 2018), and low-temperature performance (Sbihi et al., 2018) are the most common constraints reported in commercial biodiesel applications. In particular, oxidation in biodiesel has become the most serious issue among other sustainability concerns in which the oxidation of biodiesel fuel in automotive engines can produce a variety of technical problems, such as the production of insoluble gum, injector coking, fuel filter blockage, and moving engine parts sticking (Cardeño et al., 2020), and degradation of fuel system materials (Chandran et al., 2016). A report from the National Association of Corrosion Engineers (NACE) estimated the global cost of corrosion at $2.5 trillion in 2016, or 3.4% of global GDP in 2013. In major economies, corrosion losses account for 2.7% of GDP in the U.S., 4.2% in the EU, 5% in India, 4.2% in Saudi Arabia, 4% in China, 1% in Japan, and 4% in Russia. Implementing corrosion control systems could reduce these losses by 15 %–35%, translating to a global value of $375 to $875 billion annually. This sustainable issue necessitates major effort to make biodiesel more technically suitable for biodiesel engines and storage tanks (Ortega et al., 2019; El Hawary et al., 2022).

Further challenges in biodiesel storage and handling arise when the metallic surface comes into contact with the chemical environment of biodiesel, resulting in metallic corrosion via an electrochemical process (Prabhakaran et al., 2023), rusting minerals being the most common type of corrosion product (Fazal et al., 2022), followed by degradation of strength materials in industrial settings with metallic components (Al-Amiery et al., 2024). Metallic corrosion processes in biodiesel environments have developed an interest in recent decades, focusing on having a notable corrosive effect on metals because of the high sulfur component content (Pusparizkita et al., 2020). Biodiesel is frequently manufactured from vegetable oils, animal fats, or recycled cooking oil and can exhibit acidic qualities, along with being hygroscopic with free water content and becomes more corrosive in the presence of free fatty origin, so corrosion is a significant factor in many industries that affects biofuel compatibility with metallic materials (Khan et al., 2022; Shen et al., 2018; Gavrilescu, 2021; Brahma et al., 2022).

Numerous factors contribute to biodiesel's corrosiveness, predominantly because of its chemical composition, which includes fatty acid methyl esters (FAME) (Santana and Meia, 2023). One key problem is its high oxygen content, which increases during biodiesel storage (Fazal et al., 2010). Compared to traditional petroleum diesel, biodiesel contains more oxygen, which can lead to the formation of organic acids over time (Chourasia et al., 2023; Pattanaik and Misra, 2017). Acids like formic and acetic acid can accelerate the corrosion of certain metals. Additionally, biodiesel is hygroscopic, meaning it can absorb moisture from the air (Hidayat et al., 2021; Baena and Calderón, 2020). The presence of water in biodiesel can lead to the formation of corrosive acids, further increasing the risk of corrosion within the storage tank and fuel system (Fardilah et al., 2022).

Despite microbial growth, biodiesel is also susceptible to microbial contamination (Yeşilyurt et al., 2019). Microorganisms can grow in the water and biodiesel, producing corrosive byproducts that worsen corrosion (He et al., 2007). As a result, biodiesel is incompatible with metals often found in gasoline systems (Komariah et al., 2022). Biodiesel, for example, is more aggressive than standard diesel in terms of elastomers and some metals. Improper blending or storage of biodiesel might worsen its corrosive qualities (Haseeb et al., 2011). For example, biodiesel blends with higher biodiesel concentrations (e.g., B100, 100% biodiesel) can be more corrosive than lower blends (e.g., B20, 20% biodiesel) (Chandran et al., 2016). Using B100 is rare due to its high cost and limited availability. No engine manufacturers can warranty engines or components when using B100 as fuel. The most prevalent biodiesel blends are B2-to-B20. Even at low B1 or B2 blends, biodiesel is environmentally friendly and considerably improves lubricity in diesel fuel, which refers to its capacity to provide lubrication and minimize wear between moving parts in a diesel engine.

Further, mild steel storage tanks are prone to corrosion, and prevention is vital to ensuring their continued usage in the petroleum, food, electrical, chemical, and construction industries. Correspondingly, there has been a lot of interest in modifying steel surface qualities with films or coatings to improve wear and corrosion resistance. The qualities required are resistance to corrosion, oxidation, and wear. Surface coating approaches may include solid-state deposition with a chemical solution, electrochemical deposition, and sol-gel deposition, which are extensively used and successful methods for increasing the anti-corrosion properties of steel and its alloys through modified zing coatings (Al-Swaidani, 2017). Moreover, working from the point of view of corrosion inhibition, some researchers have reported the synergistic effect of zinc ions on inhibiting metal corrosion in combination with other compounds like polyphosphates (Sabzi and Arefinia, 2019). Nonetheless, the inhibitory effect of zinc ions in conjunction with CaCO3 on the corrosion of carbon steel for conventional marine corrosion protection of steel has not been extensively utilized in the biodiesel environment (Nnaji et al., 2023). Accordingly, applying protective coatings (Tasić et al., 2020), such as from calcium carbonate derived from locally available waste raw materials to steel protective coatings (Sterpu et al., 2024) and steel plating coated with zinc, are promising for corrosion protection on the metallic storage tank, exposed to the biodiesel environment (Molina et al., 2023).

Additionally, the nature of the metal surface (composition, roughness), the biodiesel environment, and correct deposition methods can all impact the performance of a protective coating (Yeşilyurt et al., 2019). Understanding the types of corrosion and the factors that influence them is critical to implementing effective corrosion prevention strategies in various industries, including construction, transportation, and manufacturing (Al-kroom et al., 2023; Kulkarni et al., 2023; Majgaonkar, 2011). To minimize the corrosive effects of biodiesel, use compatible materials in fuel system components and follow proper storage and handling procedures (Baena and Calderón, 2020; Thakur et al., 2023; Komariah et al., 2023). Furthermore, the industry has created standards and specifications to address these concerns, such as ASTM D6751 for biodiesel quality (Vaishak et al., 2023) and ASTM D7467 for biodiesel blends (Jan et al., 2023; Çakmak and Özcan, 2020). It is worth noting that biodiesel research and technological advancements are ongoing to improve its compatibility with existing infrastructure and reduce its corrosive impact. Reducing corrosion degradation of metals that coat metal surfaces can help to reduce corrosion damage. In the context of biodiesel service, there is a challenging green solution to control and inhibit corrosion on steel surfaces by developing protective coatings for mild steel with calcium carbonate composites (Thakur et al., 2023; Totis et al.).

This study electrolytically deposited on the surface of mild carbon steel with Zn-PCC composite to test the steel's corrosive susceptibility to biodiesel products. The research investigated the value of using PCC (ashbuton) precursors derived from affordable, sustainable, and locally available waste raw materials, as well as a commercially available PCC product, to create biocompatible and eco-friendly composite coatings on mild steel using electrolytic co-deposition techniques for improved surface corrosion resistance in a biodiesel-simulated setting. A range of analytical techniques such as Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), scanning electron microscopy/x-ray dispersive analysis (SEM-EDX), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, were used to evaluate the coated mild steel product in a biodiesel-simulated environment. The linear polarization electrochemical technique and the weighted mass loss method were employed to compute the corrosion rates. The investigation's findings may shed light on coated mild steel storage tanks' susceptibility to biocorrosion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Substrate preparation and biodiesel blend analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
This study used mild steel with the compound composition reported in Table 1 . The cathode of this plate steel was fabricated per ASTM G 31–72 requirements, measuring 20 mm × 20 mm x 1 mm. Following three iterations of abrasive grinding using silicon carbide paper and coarse (240), medium (600), and fine (1200) grids, all specimens were thoroughly cleaned with ethanol and water to eliminate any residual oxides from their surfaces. Subsequently, 0.01 M sodium carbonate and 10% HCl acid solution were applied to the steel sample surfaces for 10 s at an ambient temperature to clean and prepare them for electrodeposition. Instead, medium corrosion used biodiesel (B100) made from palm oil obtained from producer PT Wilmar through APROBI, an association of Indonesian biodiesel producers adhering to the ASTM D6751 standard, and Pertamina Dex (B0) to the ASTM D6304 standard. Tables 2–3
show the chemical composition information for both fuels. The two fuels were then blended at a ratio of 35 v/v to produce the B35 blend-product. Moreover, the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) (the PerkinElmer UATR) method collected infrared spectra from the absorption or emission of biodiesel blends. Thermo Nicolet FTIR Nexus spectrometer and TGS (Tri-glycine sulfate) detector were used to scan the mid-IR region 4000-400 cm−1 at a room resolution of 4 cm−1 (Errico et al., 2023; Li et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PCC powder prepared from the solid residue of extracted natural asphalt
ce:section.ce:para: The study involved preparing PCC (precipitated calcium carbonate) powder using dry solid waste residues from the extraction process of natural asphalt rock in Buton City, Indonesia. This dry solid residue was mineralized with CO2 and then precipitated calcium carbonate using the calcining-dissolving-precipitating (CDP) process in a solution rich in calcium ions (Pusparizkita et al., 2023). The calcining residue was dissolved in ammonium salt to create a calcium-rich solution, which was then mineralized with CO2 to precipitate calcium carbonate. The initial alkalinity for calcium carbonate precipitation was pH 12, which reduced to pH 7, yielding PCC powder, also known as PCC (ashbuton), employed in Zn-PCC coating composites.

Fig. 1 shows the electrodeposition setup adopted from bath composition Table 4 . The procedure for filling the bath formulation solution was according to Aigbodion's previous work (Aigbodion VS et al., 2019). The following analytical grade chemicals dissolved in 1 L of distilled water included 15 g boric acid, 10 g zinc powder, 100 g potassium chloride, and 100 g zinc chloride, and then homogenized for 24 h. The initial pH of the acid setting was 4.5. Coating the substrate with 6 g of PCC (ashbuton) from locally sourced synthesis and commercial, then mixing each component in 500 ml of electrolyte. The Zn plate anode and metal plate cathode were then submerged in a bath and connected to a DC power source. This experiment after Atikpo et al. (2022) discovered that the maximum PCC addition utilized during electrodeposition should be 10 g to facilitate maximal dissolution and avoid separation.

Electrodeposition is a well-known technique for improving surface, decorative, and functional characteristics of materials (Popoola et al., 2016; Gražulis et al., 2012). It is a flexible, low-cost method for producing coatings and films. The process applies electrochemical principles used for anti-corrosion coatings. It is also emerging as a versatile technique for nanomaterials. Fig. 2 depicts the uncoated and coated samples analyzed in the study. The operating parameters for temperature, stirring speed, and voltage were set to 25 °C, 70 rpm, and 5V, respectively. The deposit mass percentage test was then used to calculate the increase in specimen mass before and after coating. The mass % coating of Zn and Zn-PCC composite was performed using a digital scale and the formula equation shown below:
(1)

mass =

M 0

Where M1 is the coated mass sample, and Mo is the uncoated mass sample.

The study measured the mass of zinc coating deposits for various Zn-PCC coatings at different values. These mass deposits can serve as a reference for strength value, with mass transport typically controlling the process. The specific gravity of zinc powder (7.13 g/cm3) is greater than that of PCC (2.71 g/cm3). The relationship between gravity and the electric field impacts the structure of the mass deposits for Zn-PCC bonding. During the study, the average respective mass of coated Zn, Zn-ashbuton PCC, and Zn-commercial PCC samples were 1.324, 2.320, and 1.541 g.

The Surftest (SJ-210) with an LCD measured the surface roughness of workpieces. This device offers a measurement range of −200 to +160 μm, a detection range of 360 μm, and an X-axis measuring range of 16.0 mm. The handheld digital thickness gauge (UT 343D) measured coating thickness with an adjustable tolerance of ±10.0 μm and a measuring range of 0–1250 μm. The Vickers microhardness test utilized the HMV-2000 model from Shimadzu Corporation in Kyoto, Japan, and the standard ASTM 384 processing procedure to estimate the diagonal indentation.

The corrosion rate was determined using an electrochemical method (model CS300) with a 3.5% NaCl solution as the corrosive medium. The tested corrosion specimens immersed were in B35 biodiesel fuel for seven (7) days. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) examined an electrode for AC potential signals with low amplitudes (∼10 mV) over a broad frequency range. AC impedance spectroscopy is an effective method for measuring the kinetic parameters of electrode processes, such as the electrolyte and passivation layer. Charge-transfer resistance (Rct) is a parameter for quantitatively determining the rate of an electrode reaction. Faraday's law states that the corrosion rate of an anode is proportional to its corrosion current. The area weight loss typically represents the corrosion and depletion rate, with mmpy (millimeter per year) being the most commonly used unit. Equation (2) describes the relationship between current density and corrosion rate

Where the CR = corrosion rate (mmpy), Icorr = corrosion current density (μA/cm2), Ew = equivalent weight (grams/equivalent) and ρ = density (g/cm3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure analysis of the corroded surface samples
ce:section.ce:para: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU3500) and Energy Dispersion Spectroscopy (EDX) were employed to identify the individual chemical components in the corroded surface samples. Before SEM inspection, the coupon-corroded specimens were dried and placed in a desiccator. A 0.5 mm thick gold coating was added to the specimens' surfaces to provide electrical conductivity, and SEM pictures were obtained at various magnifications. Furthermore, the obtained corroded surface samples were analyzed using the XRD method to detect rusting oxide minerals on the surfaces following immersion in B35 biodiesel. The metal coating layer contaminated with B35 biodiesel was dried and desiccated, following X-ray exposure with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) and 2θ ranges (10°–90° angles). Using crystal structure models from the COD (Crystallography Open Database), the Rietveld refinement procedure with the X'Pert High Score Plus Program confirmed the crystal phases discovered by the search matching approach (Gražulis et al., 2012; Vijaya et al., 2002).

Fig. 3 shows the FTIR spectra of three (three) distinct FAME Biodiesel products combined with Pertamina-Dex diesel oil. The peak spectra assignments are in Table 5 . Bands between 3346 and 2816 cm−1 may correspond to the carboxylic acid's O–H stretch. Two sharp bonds at 2922 and 2853 cm−1, overlaid on the O–H stretch, were assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric CH2-stretches, respectively. The sharp peak at 1710 cm−1 indicates the presence of the CO stretch, whereas the band at 1285 cm−1 indicates the presence of the C–O stretch (Smith, 1999). The O–H in-plane and out-of-plane bonds are represented by spectra at 1401.38 and 937 cm−1, respectively (Zhang, 2012). All mixtures from B0 to B100 have carbon-carbon (C–C) and carbon-hydrogen (C–H) bonds at 2952, 2922, and 2853 cm−1. Standard FAME biodiesel usually has a C–H bond at 2924 cm−1, connected with the Ester's alkyl group. Furthermore, another peak at 2853 cm−1 corresponds to the asymmetric methyl group (Ferreras and Miller, 2023). The unique signal for the B100 product corresponds to the CO carbonyl bond (1741 cm−1), implying the presence of ester groups (Akinfalabi et al., 2020). The peak of the B35 product at 1744 cm−1 is due to the asymmetric vibration of the C–H bond and the existence of ester lipids in the three combinations (Gonzalez Caranton et al., 2021). Other peaks detected in B100 and B35 products were at 722 cm−1, 1436 cm−1, and 1374 cm−1 with paraffin with methyl-type vibrations (Lam and Lee, 2011), and the O–CH3 band at 1169 cm−1 corresponds to mono-alkyl esters with asymmetric axial vibration stretching (Pimentel et al., 2006). These band peaks reveal capacity and resemblance, indicating that FAME Biodiesel may be a feasible blend with petroleum diesel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface hardness, roughness and thickness coating
ce:section.ce:para: Surface hardness controls durability by protecting the substrate and itself from wear and other mechanical stressors; thus, hardness provides superior barrier characteristics against external forces. Moisture, chemicals, temperature, and dust affect coating thickness and performance. As the coating's thickness decreases, it becomes more resistant to deformation on a substrate. Keeping all factors constant throughout the process, such as curing time and application conditions, results in a more damage-resistant system. Thus, thinner films have higher hardness than thicker ones. These characteristics include electrical, optical, cracking, and corrosion resistance.

The surface properties are present in Table 6 . The samples' hardness values increased from the uncoated mild steel coated with Zn and Zn-PCC to mild steel. The coating layers had an average hardness value of 101.5, 113.67, 122.33, and 129.33 HV. Coating Zn-PCC composites in the mild steel samples contributes to the hardness value of mild steel (Mostafa and El-Gendy, 2017). The composite layer's surface hardness is high due to a phase at the interface, which results in a high dislocation density on the surface layer. After applying the Zn–CaCO3 double layer, the uncoated hardness values increased. However, the hardness variation (within standard errors) between PCC (ashbuton) and PCC (commercial) may be related to particle size and impurities created when PCC is manufactured from asphalt waste residue. Previous studies have shown that increased microhardness relates to the development of a phase, reduced grain size, denser organization, and thicker layers (Xavier, 2022). Aigbodion et al. (Aigbodion VS et al., 2019) reported an increase in hardness value of 90.17% related to adding Zn-20g/PCC on the influence of mechanical strength, roughness, and layer thickness. In general, the formation of Zn-PCC composites increased the hardness of both layers relative to the Zn layer. Instead, the increase in the single layer of Zn is due to the smaller particle size. However, the Zn-PCC (commercial) layer generated after the initial coating procedure contributes to a structure that increases resistance to dislocation movement. Many studies have found links between hardness and corrosion. According to much research, corrosion resistance increases with increasing hardness due to finer grain size and higher martensite content on the metallic substrates. In the study, the thickness of the Zn, Zn-PCC (ashbuton), and Zn-PCC (commercial) layers were 12.1, 17.5, and 17.26 μm, respectively. The thickness of coating layers generated from Zn-PCC composites varies depending on the processing parameters (Xavier, 2023). The rise in thickness of the surface layer following the first layer is related to the added thick layer to the initial Zn layer.

Surface coatings are a popular and effective way to prevent metal corrosion by limiting the metal's exposure to corrosive conditions. In this study, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) approach qualitatively examined the electrochemical kinetics of corroded samples, resulting in Nyquist plots (Fig. 4 a). The semicircle diameters of the Zn-coating, base metal, and Zn-PCC (ashbuton) samples were broader than those of the Zn-PCC (commercial) sample in the high to medium frequency range. Furthermore, the low-frequency slope suggested that the coated Zn-PCC (ashbuton) samples had superior charge transfer kinetics, which led to a drop in electron transport resistance as the coating thickness rose. Only the curves for Zn-PCC (ashbuton) samples showed an increased trend in the low-frequency area compared to uncoated mild steel. The Zn-PCC (commercial) sample exhibited a big semicircle diameter in the high-frequency area for the lowest interfacial charge resistance. Resistance reduction is as the amount of Zn-PCC (ashbuton) powder in the coating increases, supported by the low-frequency trends.

Further, electrical equivalent circuits (Fig. 4b and c) show Rs, Rc, and Rct, representing solution, coating, and charge transfer resistances, respectively. The polarization resistance of Rp = Rct + Rc. Furthermore, Rs represents the ion adsorption and desorption resistances at the interface and the induction parameter. CPEc and CPEct are non-ideal capacitors that use constant phase elements like coating and charge transfer. The constant for phase elements has two parameters: n and Y0, which indicate the components and admittance of CPE, respectively. Furthermore, each CPE system enhances the solution's passivity (Yo, n). In this investigation, the value of n predicts whether the inhibitor is capacitive or resistant. Several n closer to 0 improves the inhibitor resistance, while an interval of 0–1 increases the inhibitor's capacitance (Akande et al., 2018).

The Nyquist plot findings and the influence of the computed EIS parameter are in Table 7 , showing the Rct values of corrosion resistance on materials coated with Zn, Zn-PCC (ashbuton), and Zn-PCC (commercial), recorded at 2454 Ω cm2, 3686 Ω cm2, and 907.2 Ω cm2, respectively. Varying Rct values may be related to Zn-coatings and Zn-PCC composites formed by electrodeposition with different PCC particle concentrations. Existing PCC particles to surface coatings may regulate their morphological, structural, and corrosion resistance. The EIS examination confirmed that increased PCC particle content enhances corrosion protection, with the highest (Rp) polarization resistance of 4204.6 Ω cm2 achieved in the Zn-PCC (ashbuton) coating. The Rp value, in particular, may diminish after more than 7 days of immersion. As a result, the following experiment may show damage in the surface coating and electrolyte caused by pores penetrating the coating. In general, higher Rp indicates superior corrosion resistance.

Further, the computed Chi-squared value should be low (χ2 < 0.001), and the current values are still higher than those reported in the literature (Xavier, 2023; Akande et al., 2018). As a result, it should not be an exclusive consideration when determining the optimal match. Model literature may have a different actual system, with the elements chosen to address any physical processes occurring at the electrode/electrolyte contact. It might be in anticipating comparable circuits with some diagnostic tests performed before EIS. These could include the linear polarization resistance trend and the low potential regime polarization trend for calculating Rct and Y0 (Xavier, 2023; Akande et al., 2018).

Apparently, the Zn-PCC (ashbuton) composite showed better film thickness and corrosion resistance than those for standard zinc coatings. The corrosion rate (CR) is directly related to polarization resistance (Rp), calculated by combining Rc and Rct values; specimens with lower Rp are more prone to corrosion, according to Equation (2). The inclusion of CaCO3 showed a significant rise in the alloy's capacitance arc radius. Compared to the Zn coating, the Zn–CaCO3 (Ashburton) composite coating has a higher film layer thickness and Rct values and better corrosion resistance. Accordingly, corrosion resistance for coated mild steel using Zn, Zn-PCC (ashbuton), and Zn-PCC (commercial) shows the following sequence: Zn-PCC (commercial) > Zn > Zn-PCC (ashbuton). Corrosion resistance in Nyquist plots increases with increasing the capacitive loop or semicircle diameter. This result could be attributed to the substantial presence of zinc in the coating, as revealed by the EDX composition study (Table 8 ).

The corrosion protection ability of the composite coatings was estimated as the coating efficiency (C
eff ) (Khodair et al., 2018; Dezfuli and Sabzi, 2019) relative to the corrosion rate using the relationship in Eqn. (3).
(3)

C c − C o

Where C0 is the corrosion rate of the uncoated steel specimen, and Cc is the corrosion rate of the composite coated steel specimen. The uncoated substrate exhibited the lowest polarization resistance of 1482.25Ω and the highest corrosion rate of 6.1161E-7 mm/year (Table 7). The Zn-coated sample has a corrosion rate of 3.5997E-7 mm/year and a coating efficiency of 99.9%. Consequently, the predicted coating efficiencies of all Zn–CaCO3-coated steel samples were correspondingly low. However, all Zn–CaCO3 coated steel samples had lower corrosion rates than the control (uncoated steel). In this study, the Electrolytic Co-deposition of Zn and Zn–CaCO3 composite may generate a calcium carbonate coating with good adherence and anticorrosive properties, as shown by the highest (Rp) polarization resistance that can prevent aggressive Cl ions from penetrating Ca metal surfaces, reducing corrosion processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM and EDX analysis of corrosion-tested coated samples
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of the surface Zn and Zn-PCC coated steel revealed the presence of pores (shown by the red arrows) in the SEM picture in Fig. 5 c and d. The porosity in a surface coating of the moisture and electrolyte species penetrates the underlying substrate, potentially affecting the coating's corrosion protection efficacy. According to EIS study findings, Zn and Zn-PCC coated mild steel exhibited the lowest corrosion rate in the biodiesel environment compared to uncoated steel because the hard-coated surface prevents biodiesel from flowing through, resulting in reduced corrosion. The uncoated mild steel, Zn-coated, Zn-PCC (ashbuton), and Zn-PCC (commercial) coated mild steels demonstrate considerable morphological changes shown in SEM images (Fig. 5). Corrosion dramatically changed the morphology of metal surfaces, except mild steel surfaces, which were less affected when exposed to biodiesel. The Zn and Zn-PCC-coated steel samples had a black coating on their surfaces. Organic salts or oxides may be the source of corrosion on such coated sample surfaces. There was less iron oxide, but more organic salt was on the surface of mild carbon steel and all Zn and Zn-PCC-coated surfaces.

The appearance changed less than the Zn-coated surface, although the entire Zn-coated surface became significantly darker. The appearance of all Zn-PCC-coated samples was consistent. The SEM image (Fig. 5a) of the uncoated mild steel has a smooth surface, whereas the Zn-coated mild steel has pores (represented by the red lines) in the SEM image (Fig. 5b). Zn-PCC-coated mild steel samples have uniformly distributed pores (shown by the red lines) compared to Zn-coated steel samples (Fig. 5c and d). However, the remaining impurities cause an inhomogeneous size component in the particle structure, which prevents the particle grains from undergoing the correct sizing procedure. The existing pores in the surface coating make moisture and electrolyte species infiltrate the underlying substrate, reducing the coating's corrosion protection efficacy. As a result, examining the porosity of a surface coating is critical when determining its corrosion protection capacity. Some oxidation products enter biodiesel as ions or deposits, while others react with free fatty acids to produce fatty acid salts on metal surfaces. These factors may explain why biodiesel and metal surfaces have different appearances.

Table 8 shows a microelement analysis of the SEM image given. The uncoated mild steel samples have a high Fe concentration, which adds to the steel composition. Furthermore, the existing Zn, C, and O in Zn-coated samples correspond to the amount of Zn and potential scale growth of Ca, C, and O in the Zn-coated surface sample. Zn-PCC (ashbuton) and Zn-PCC (commercial) coatings had significant levels of Zn, C, O, and Ca, similar to the coating for a solid solution of Zn–CaCO3 composite. Similar discoveries on the presence of Ca, C, and O are consistent with previous research findings (Zhao et al., 2020; Adams et al., 2022). High quantities of Zn, iron, carbon, and oxygen produced a solid solution in which certain Fe2+ cations were replaced by Ca2+, resulting in a mixed carbonate (FexCa1-xCO3). The corrosion and scaling products generated in B35-biodiesel solutions found in EDX are 0.8–0.97 % iron and 0.75–3.21 % calcium as a function of the CO2 exposure period. Instead, potassium (K) and sulfur (S) present in the mild steel, Zn, and Zn–CaCO3 coating EDX results could be due to ambient contamination acquired during sample handling before SEM examination or after electrodeposition. The presence of sulfur in zinc and Zn–CaCO3 coatings may create harmful compounds with zinc, promoting high surface corrosion rates in the substrate (Wang et al., 2023). Furthermore, the substrate will degrade more quickly if corrosion products occur at the substrate/coating interface rather than the coating/electrolyte border (Smith et al., 2017).

The XRD analyses of the samples shown are in Fig. 6 . The XRD of the uncoated and coated mild steel is comparable to the EDX composition of the mild steel reported previously. The uncoated mild alloy steel has three peaks at α-Fe of 44.41, 64.74°, and 82.08° as the main constituents of the steel. Additionally, samples coated with Zn powder included unreacted Zn phases. The X-ray diffraction pattern confirmed that the undetected oxide phase in the corrosion rust formed in the Zn-coated sample. In this case, the Zn-PCC coating completely covered the mild steel surface, leaving only the Zn-PCC diffraction peaks visible. In the present study, corrosion-resistant coatings made using varied PCC crystals (ashbuton and commercial) for mild steel coating provided better corrosion protection since there is no iron oxide, a rusting mineral, as revealed in XRD data.

Long-term storage, high temperatures, moisture absorption due to its hygroscopic nature, and metal contamination can all influence biodiesel properties. Corrosion of metallic equipment in biodiesel plants impedes biodiesel storage and delivery to end users. In the present situation, mild steel utilized in biodiesel storage tanks, due to its good formability, availability, low cost, and remarkable mechanical properties, requires further surface treatment to prevent the high corrosion rate when in contact with biodiesels. As a result, an effort has improved mild steel corrosion resistance in a biodiesel environment using single and double layers of Zn–CaCO3 coatings (Atikpo et al., 2022). However, the long-term use of CaCO3 in mild steel coating becomes a high expense of CaCO3, as limestone is the primary material used in Portland cement production (Refait et al., 2017). As a result, there is an urgent need to recover CaCO3 from waste to minimize the cost of coating materials. Previous research has described a potential innovative mild steel coating employing CaCO3 obtained from eggshell waste and the co-deposition process to develop Zn– CaCO3 (single and double layer) (Atikpo et al., 2022). The current work addressed using a PCC percussor made from recovered asphalt-solid waste with a tailored structure for coating mild steel in a biodiesel storage tank. The coated Zn-PCC (ashbuton) samples exhibited a lower corrosion rate in the biodiesel environment than the other uncoated and coated samples, as evidenced by the hard-coated surface, which prevented biodiesel from passing through and caused less corrosion damage. According to the Classification of General and Pitting Corrosion Rates (NACE SP0775-2013), Zn-PCC (ashbuton) has a general corrosion rate of less than 0.025 mm per year, making it an appropriate coating material for mild steel biodiesel storage tanks (Umoru et al., 2012). This innovative coating can be used in the industrial fabrication of biodiesel storage tanks because the ashbuton PCC percussor can be produced in a simple chemical process, resulting in a suitable conversion coating that provides an adherent and protective layer on a mild steel substrate (Pusparizkita et al., 2020; Hoang et al., 2020).

In this research, the main conclusions obtained through experimental measurements and theoretical calculations are as follows:
1.
Zn and PCC powder composites were successfully formed on mild steel using the electrodeposition coating method
2.
The results of immersion with the B35 coating layer for 30 days did not affect the occurrence of corrosion; however, using the EIS method through graph plots and resistance against frequency shows the results of values that can predict the occurrence of corrosion to prepare for handling and maintenance management in storage tank applications biodiesel made of mild steel. XRD and SEM reveal no iron oxides developed in the Zn and Zn-PCC coated steel samples.
3.
The Zn-coating may be employed in the industrial fabrication of biodiesel storage tanks made of steel alloy, which is extremely promising from this study and may have the potential for the fuel storage industry to improve its corrosion resistance. According to electrochemical results, coating materials with Zn-PCC have a lower corrosion rate than Zn-coated samples.
